elements.sent-2411-105: We use a point cloud-based deep learning model to deal with the galaxy distribution.
elements.sent-2411-106: A point cloud is a set of points represented by vectors, and the vectors are generally described in a three-dimensional space with additional information.
elements.sent-2411-107: In the field of information science, point clouds mainly represent the surface of an object obtained by devices such as Light Detection And Ranging (LiDAR) sensors \citep[e.g.,][]{Geiger2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-108: The merit of point clouds is the ability to represent sparse data more efficiently than voxels or meshes.
elements.sent-2411-109: In protocluster detection, it is difficult to integrate the information of each galaxy into voxels, and the distribution of galaxies is very sparse compared to the size of the universe.
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, it is desirable to analyze the galaxy distribution by representing them as a point cloud.
elements.sent-2411-111: PointNet \citep{Qi2016 is a point cloud-based deep learning model, which is the foundation of recent point cloud analysis with deep learning.}
elements.sent-2411-112: PointNet achieved higher performance than a conventional 3D fully convolutional Network, which is processed by voxelizing and convolutional neural networks, by adopting a network structure that considers the symmetry of the order-invariance and transform-invariance of point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-113: DG-CNN \citep{Wang2018 is one of the successor models of PointNet, which takes localization in the network by dynamically computed $k$-neighbor graphs in each network layer.}
elements.sent-2411-114: We propose a three-step approach to detect protocluster candidates: First (section \ref{sssec:mdn ), we preprocess the input galaxy data with the Mixture Density Network \citep{Bishop1994 to estimate the redshift probability density function (PDF) with three optical band magnitudes ($g,\,r,\,i$).}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Secondly (section \ref{sssec:pcfnet ), we use the new deep-learning model, PCFNet, to predict the probability that the target galaxy is a protocluster member.}

elements.sent-2411-154: We develop a new point cloud-based deep learning model as a protocluster finder, PCFNet, inspired by PointNet and DG-CNN.
elements.sent-2411-155: PCFNet comprises the following four parts: a prior layer, skipDG blocks, a global max pooling layer, and a classifier (Figure \ref{fig:pcfnet ).}
elements.sent-2411-156: In the prior layer, the input features of each galaxy are expanded to 16 dimensions, which is a combination of a pointwise convolutional layer, batch normalization, and Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU, \citealt{Glorot2011 ) as an activation function.}
elements.sent-2411-157: The first half of the skipDG block consists of EdgeConv \citep{Wang2018 , batch normalization, and ReLU, which output is then combined with the original input of the skipDG block.}
elements.sent-2411-158: EdgeConv plays a role in capturing the local features of the point cloud: EdgeConv first constructs a local neighborhood graph, $\mathcal{G ^l$, from point clouds, $\mathcal{P ^l\subseteq\mathbb{R ^{N\times C_l}$, which $l$ is an index of EdgeConv, $C_l$ is the dimension of input matrix of the $l$th EdgeConv, and $N$ is the number of points.}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Then, $K$ neighborhoods, $\mathbf{x ^l_{i_k}\ (1\leq k\leq K)$ are selected by each point, $\mathbf{x ^l_i \ (1\leq i\leq N)$, and new feature vectors, $\mathbf{x ^l_{(i,k)}\in \mathbb{R ^{2C_l}$, are made as the following: \begin{equation}\mathbf{x}^l_{(i,k)}=\left( \begin{matrix} \mathbf{x}^l_{i} \mathbf{x}^l_{i_k} - \mathbf{x}^l_{i} \end{matrix} \right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-160: After that, the pointwise convolution, $h_\Theta$, and global pooling among the neighborhood features are applied to the vectors: \begin{equation}h_\Theta\left(x^l_{(i,k)}\right) = ReLU\left(\Theta x^l_{(i,k)}\right) %\in R^{C_{l+1}\times K} x^{l+1}_{i} = \max_{1\leq k\leq K} \left\{h_\Theta\left(x^l_{(i,k)}\right)\right\},\end{equation} where $\Theta\in\mathbb{R ^{C_{l+1}\times2C_l}$ is the shared weights of the pointwise convolution.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}h_\Theta\left(x^l_{(i,k)}\right) = ReLU\left(\Theta x^l_{(i,k)}\right) %\in R^{C_{l+1}\times K} x^{l+1}_{i} = \max_{1\leq k\leq K} \left\{h_\Theta\left(x^l_{(i,k)}\right)\right\},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-161: The padding features are replaced by a small enough value to mask meaningless values when global pooling.
elements.sent-2411-162: The second half of the skipDG block is the network of sequences; pointwise convolution layer, batch normalization, and ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-163: Four stacked skipDG blocks enhanced the features to $16\rightarrow32\rightarrow64\rightarrow128\rightarrow256$.
elements.sent-2411-164: The global max pooling layer compresses the pointcloud features into a 256-dimensional vector.
elements.sent-2411-165: The role of the classifier is to reduce the dimensions of the vector ($256\rightarrow128\rightarrow64\rightarrow1$) to output a probability that the target galaxy is a protocluster member.
elements.sent-2411-166: The classifier consists of several layers in the order of the dense layer, batch normalization, and ReLU.
elements.sent-2411-167: A dropout layer is sandwiched between the second and third classifier layers, and dropout is performed during training with a probability of $p=0.3$.
elements.sent-2411-168: The sigmoid function is used as the activation function for the final layer.

elements.sent-2411-169: We input the following values of all galaxies (say $N$ from the following) in a radius of $5'$ around the target galaxy; the sky coordinates, $d_{\mathrm{samp }$, $i$ band magnitude, $(g-i)$, and the nine parameters of the probability distribution described above.}

elements.sent-2411-175: The cross-entropy error is used as the loss function: \begin{equation}E=-\dfrac{1}{N_b}\sum_{i=1}^{N_b}\left(y_i\log{p_i} + \left(1-y_i\right)\log{\left(1-p_i\right)}\right),\end{equation} where $N_b$ is the size of the mini-batch, $\mathbf{y =\left(y_1, \dots,y_{N_{b }\right)^\top$ is the one-hot vector for being a protocluster member galaxy, and $\mathbf{p =\left(p_1, \dots, p_{N_{b }\right)^\top$ is the output of PCFNet.}}}}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Apply PCFNet to the HSC-SSP Deep and UltraDeep
elements.sent-2411-241: We apply PCFNet to the observational data, HSC-SSP Deep, and obtain the protocluster member probabilities for each galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-242: One thing should be noted before applying it to observed data: unlike simulated data, actual observation data has masked regions.
elements.sent-2411-243: Masked areas reduce the number of galaxies, which in turn reduces the performance of PCFNet and decreases the protocluster member probability.
elements.sent-2411-244: To quantify the effect, we first make the mock mask regions on the simulation data.
elements.sent-2411-245: The mask regions of the COSMOS UltraDeep region, which has the same field of view as the PCcone light cone, are directly converted to mock mask regions on PCcone.
elements.sent-2411-246: We exclude PCcone galaxies that are within $10^{-4}$ arcmin of the nearest random object in the mock mask region.
elements.sent-2411-247: For each target galaxy, the masked area fraction is calculated from the number of random objects within a five arcmin radius of the galaxy, and target galaxies with a fraction of 50\% or less are excluded.
elements.sent-2411-248: We then compute the ratio of the protocluster member probabilities between the mock data with and without mask regions as a function of the masked area fraction (Figure \ref{fig:maskaffect ).}
elements.sent-2411-249: As expected, the probability decreases as the mask fraction increases, although the variance is large.
elements.sent-2411-250: We approximate this function with a cubic function to adjust the protocluster member probability.
elements.sent-2411-251: Protoclusters whose masked area fraction exceeds 30\% are excluded because the correction is too large.

elements.sent-2411-350: We develop a new protocluster detection method by deep learning and detect 121 protocluster candidates at $z\sim4$ from HSC-SSP Deep/UltraDeep layer.
elements.sent-2411-351: We also discuss the properties of detected protocluster from both simulational and observational views.
elements.sent-2411-352: The main contributions of this paper are the following:.
elements.sent-2411-353: We develop a new deep learning model, PCFNet which is based on PointNet \citep{Qi2016 to handle pointcloud such as galaxy distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-354: PCFNet uses relative positions, magnitude, and colors of galaxies within $5'$ around a target galaxy to estimate the probability that the target belongs to a protocluster.
elements.sent-2411-355: We test PCFNet with the semi-analytic model, PCcone \citep{Araya-Araya2021 to achieve the precision and recall are $7.5\pm0.2\%$ and $44\pm1\%$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-356: Based on the PCFNet output for each galaxy, protocluster candidates are detected by peak detection from the distribution of protocluster member galaxy candidates with significance greater than $\sigma_\mathrm{th = 2.5$.}
elements.sent-2411-357: The completeness and purity are $10.9\pm0.8\%$ and $69\pm4\%$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-358: We apply PCFNet to the HSC-SSP S20A Deep and UltraDeep layers and detect 121 unique protoclusters at $z\sim4$ from $\sim17.6\mathrm{\,deg^2}$.
elements.sent-2411-359: Comparing the halo mass distribution of protocluster candidates in the simulation data, PCFNet detects $7\pm2$ times more protoclusters with $M_\mathrm{halo ^{z=0 >10^{15 M_\odot h^{-1}$ than previous works which are affected by projection effects and especially 23 lower-mass protoclusters ($M_{\mathrm{halo ^{z=0 = 10^{14.0-14.5 M_\odot$) among which the previous method detects only one in evaluation data.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: We find that the distribution of detected protocluster members is skewed towards brighter and higher in terms of rest-UV luminosity, SFR, and stellar mass.
elements.sent-2411-361: The distribution of the rest-UV luminosity of the detected protocluster candidates in observational data also has a bright-end excess, which is consistent with previous observational studies.
elements.sent-2411-362: This suggests that star formation is already active in protoclusters at $z > 4$.
elements.sent-2411-363: We find the fraction of the core galaxies associated with QGs increases in dependence on halo mass at both $z=0$ and $z=4$ from simulation data.
elements.sent-2411-364: The protocluster candidates detected in this study are expected to be used for the assessment of environmental effects in protoclusters at high redshift with spectroscopic follow-up observation conducted in the future.
elements.sent-2411-365: Furthermore, they can be analyzed for each physical property by SED fitting by matching multi-wavelength data, as in \citet{Toshikawa2024 , which will derive detailed discussions with star formation history.}
elements.sent-2411-366: PCFNet can be applied to the HSC-SSP Wide layer, LSST, and other extraordinarily wider surveys with the next-generation telescopes such as Euclid, TMT, Roman, and more protoclusters are expected to be detected in the future.
elements.sent-2411-367: Moreover, if a new and improved lightcone model based on detailed hydro-dynamical simulation and photometric predictions that are even closer to reality becomes available in the future, we can expect further improvements in PCFNet with updated training data.
elements.sent-2411-368: In terms of computational resources, a single GPU is sufficient to execute the inference of PCFNet over such a vast survey area in a realistic amount of time (several minutes to several hours), and further acceleration is also easily achieved through parallelization.
elements.sent-2411-369: Furthermore, the versatility of PCFNet also makes it applicable to other dropout galaxies, and it enables us to search for protoclusters over a wide range of redshifts from $z = 2\mathchar`-8$.
